questions.question: Plotly Express (px) vs Plotly Graph Objects (go):
options: px: high-level, concise code (like seaborn). go: full control, more verbose (like matplotlib axes)
questions.explanation: Use px for quick charts, go when you need precise control or custom traces.

questions.question: The animation_frame parameter in Plotly Express creates:
options: An animated chart with a Play button -- each unique value of the column becomes one animation frame
questions.explanation: px.bar(df, animation_frame='year') creates a Play button that steps through each year.

questions.question: make_subplots() in Plotly is used for:
options: Creating a figure with multiple panels (subplots) arranged in a grid
questions.explanation: from plotly.subplots import make_subplots then add traces with row=1, col=1 etc.

questions.question: fig.show() in a Jupyter notebook does what?
options: Renders an interactive HTML chart inline -- users can hover, zoom, and click
questions.explanation: Plotly renders interactive charts directly in Jupyter. fig.write_html('chart.html') saves for sharing.

questions.question: hover_data=['country','growth'] in px.scatter adds:
options: Tooltips showing country and growth when hovering over each point
questions.explanation: hover_data controls what appears in the tooltip popup when you hover over data points.

questions.question: fig.update_layout(template='plotly_white') does what?
options: Applies a clean white theme -- removes the default grey background
questions.explanation: Common templates: plotly_white, plotly_dark, seaborn, ggplot2. Overrides the default grey.

questions.question: px.choropleth() creates:
options: A geographic map with countries coloured by a numeric variable
questions.explanation: Choropleth = countries/regions filled with colour representing data values. Use locationmode='country names' for country names.

questions.question: The size parameter in px.scatter creates:
options: Proportional bubble sizes -- data point markers sized by a numeric column
questions.explanation: size='revenue' makes each bubble's area proportional to revenue. A third visual dimension beyond x and y.

questions.question: fig.write_html('dashboard.html') is useful because:
options: Creates a standalone interactive HTML file anyone can open in a browser -- no Python needed to view
questions.explanation: The HTML file includes all Plotly.js inline. Share it like a document -- fully interactive without any server.

questions.question: Plotly Dash is for:
options: Building full interactive web dashboards with Python only -- dropdowns, sliders, callbacks, no JavaScript needed
questions.explanation: Dash = Flask + React + Plotly. Full web app framework. pip install dash.
